title: Built an AI system that scores news for my 5-stock watchlist (-1/0/+1). 2 months running automated. Here's what I learned.
author: Outside_Original_734
selftext:
Two months ago I got tired of opening 50 tabs every morning to read news on the 5 stocks I track. So I built a system that does it for me.



Stack is simple:

\- [Make.com](http://Make.com) (one scenario per ticker)

\- OpenAI gpt-4o-mini for scoring

\- Google News RSS as input

\- Google Sheet as the dashboard



Every morning at 7 AM the system pulls news, sends each article to OpenAI to score it on -1/0/+1 with an action recommendation ("Research" or "Monitor"), and writes everything to a sheet. I scan it over coffee in 30 seconds. No more rabbit holes.



\*\*What surprised me about results:\*\*



The scoring isn't perfect, but it's correlated enough with actual price movements to be useful. When the system flags multiple "Research" signals on the same ticker within 48 hours, the stock usually moves. I've stopped trusting individual scores and started trusting clusters.



\*\*Cost:\*\* \~$3/month in OpenAI tokens. Free tier on [Make.com](http://Make.com) is enough.



\*\*Time to build:\*\* \~6 hours over a few weekends. Stable for 2 months, no failed runs.



\*\*What I'd improve:\*\*

\- Currently output is JSON-as-text in one column. Refactoring to structured columns this week.

\- Running 5 separate scenarios is wasteful. Should consolidate with a router.

\- No backtesting yet — I want to validate the AI scores against actual returns over the next quarter.



\*\*Two screenshots:\*\*

1. The [Make.com](http://Make.com) workflow (RSS → OpenAI → Sheets)

2. The output sheet showing scored news for BLD, PLTR, UUU



\*\*Question for the community:\*\*

Anyone here doing systematic AI scoring on watchlists? What's your prompt structure? I'm curious whether others have found ways to make scores more consistent across articles.



Built this entirely solo over 2 months, never showed it to anyone until yesterday. Trying to break that habit.

comments.author: Cnye36
comments.body: This is a solid practical automation build. The point about trusting clusters more than single article scores makes a lot of sense. If you want to make it more stable, I’d probably separate extraction from scoring: first normalize each article into structured events/facts, then run a second pass that scores only those. That usually reduces prompt drift a lot. Backtesting those clustered “Research” bursts against a fixed 24h/48h return window would be really interesting, and probably more useful than judging one article at a time.

replies.author: Outside_Original_734
replies.body:
Really appreciate this — the two-pass approach is exactly what I've been 

circling around but couldn't articulate.



Right now I'm doing extraction + scoring in a single prompt, which works 

\~80% of the time but breaks on edge cases (long articles with multiple 

tickers, earnings reports with mixed signals). Prompt drift is the right 

word for it.



How are you handling the extraction schema? Fixed JSON schema with strict 

fields, or letting the model decide what's relevant per article? I imagine 

the trade-off is rigidity (cleaner data but missed nuance) vs flexibility 

(messier but captures rare events).



On backtesting — totally agree. I have \~2 months of scored data and price 

history for the 5 tickers I track. The honest answer is I haven't built 

the analysis pipeline yet because I was scared of finding out the scores 

are noise. Need to just do it. Probably this week.



Have you tried this on your own setup? Curious if you've seen the cluster 

effect hold up against returns. Honestly, this is the first real engagement I've gotten on something I've 

built. Posted on r/automation first — got 800 views, zero comments. 

Thanks for actually reading and responding. Means more than you'd think.
